per_page_text: Self Learning Activity — 3 Handwritten Report from NPTEL Video Residue at Simple Pole (Calculation) Subject Complex Analysis / Engineering Mathematics Topic Residue at Simple Pole — Calculation https://nptel.ac.in/courses/111/107/111107132/ Wkbo Uns (NPTEL: Complex Analysis, Prof. P. A. S. Sree Krishna) Professor Prof. P. A. S. Sree Krishna Institute IIT Guwahati Student Name Roll Number Branch Semester Date Submitted in partial fulfilment of the course requirement.

1. Introduction The concept of the residue of a complex function is one of the most powerful tools in complex analysis. It forms the backbone of the Residue Theorem, which allows us to evaluate complicated real and complex integrals by computing residues at isolated singularities. This report focuses specifically on computing the residue at a simple pole (a pole of order 1). 2. Background & Definitions 2.1 Analytic Function A function f(z) is said to be analytic at a point z = zl if it is differentiable at every point in some neighbourhood of zill. A function analytic everywhere in the complex plane is called entire (e.g., e”, sin z, polynomials). 2.2 Isolated Singularity A point zl is called an isolated singularity of f(z) if f is not analytic at zl but is analytic in some punctured neighbourhood 0 < |z — zi] < R. 2.3 Laurent Series In a punctured neighbourhood of an isolated singularity zl, f(z) can be expanded in a Laurent series: inf inf Sum a_n(z-z0)“*n + Sum b_n / (z-z0)“n n=0 n=1 The second sum is the Principal Part of f at z0. 2.4 Classification of Isolated Singularities Removable singularity Allb_n=0 sin(z)/z at z=0 Pole of order m b_m+#0,b_n=0Oforn>m 1/(z-1)? at z=1 Simple Pole (m = 1) b_1#0,b n=Oforn>1 1/(z-a) Essential singularity Infinitely many b_n #0 e(1/z) at z=0

3. Residue — Definition & Key Theorems 3.1 Definition of Residue Let zl be an isolated singularity of f(z) and let the Laurent expansion about zilli be: . + b_2/(z-z0)*2 + b_1/(z-z0) + a_O + a_1(z-z0) +... The residue of f at zl is defined as: Res[f, z0] i.e., the coefficient of 1/(z - z0) in the Laurent series. 3.2 Residue Theorem (Statement) If f(z) is analytic inside and on a simple closed positively-oriented contour C except at finitely many isolated singularities Zl, ZMH, ..., Zl inside C, then: (closed integral over C) £(z) dz = 2*pi*i * Sum Res[f, z_k] k=1 ton 4. Formulae for Residue at a Simple Pole A simple pole is a pole of order 1. There are three standard formulae used to compute the residue at a simple pole. Formula 1 — Direct Limit If zO is a simple pole of f(z), then: Res[f, zO] = lim_{z -> z0O} (z-—- z0O) * £(z) lf This is the most general formula and always works for a simple pole. Formula 2 — Quotient Form f(z) = p(z)/q(z) If f(z) = p(z)/q(z) where p and q are analytic, p(zlll) # 0, q(zll) = 0 and q'(zM) # 0 (i.e. Zl is a simple zero of q), then: Res[f, z0] = p(z0) / q'(z0) lf This is the quickest formula when f is already in p(z)/q(z) form. |
Formula 3 — Partial Fraction / Laurent Coefficient If {(z) = g(z)/(z — zl) where g is analytic at Zl and g(zM) + 0: Res[f, z0] = g(z0) (since £(z) = g(z0)/(z-z0) + analytic terms)

5. Solved Examples Example 1 — Residue of f(z) = 1 / [(z — 1)(z — 3)] at each simple pole Given: f(z) = 1 /[(z- 1)(z - 3)] Step 1 — Identify the poles. Setting the denominator to zero: (z-1)(z2-3)=O0Mz=1 andz=3. Both are simple poles (each is a zero of order 1 of the denominator). Step 2 — Residue at z = 1 using Formula 1: 1] lim_{z->1} (z-1) * 1/[(z-1) (z-3)] lim_{z->1} 1/(z-3) 1/ (1-3) 1/(-2) = -1/2 Step 3 — Residue at z = 3 using Formula 1: Res[f, 3] lim_{z->3} (z-3) * 1/[(z-1) (z-3)] lim_{z->3} 1/(z-1) 1/(3-1) = 1/2 — Verification: Sum of residues = -1/2 + 1/2 = 0. Consistent with partial fractions. Example 2 — Residue of f(z) = ez / (z2 + 1) at each simple pole Given: f(z) = 7 / (z2 + 1) = e7/[(z + i)(z—i)] Step 1 — Identify the poles. z?+1=0Mz = +i. Both are simple poles. Step 2-— Use Formula 2 (p(z) = e”, q(z) = z2 + 1, q'(z) = 2z): Res[f, i] = p(i) / q' (i) e*i / (2i) Using Euler's formula: e%i cos(1) + i*sin(1) Res[f, i] (cos 1+ i sin 1) / (2i) (cos 1)/(2i) + (i sin 1)/(2i) (cos 1)/(2i) + (sin 1)/2 (sin 1)/2 - i(cos 1)/2
Res[f, -i] = p(-i) / q'(-i) = e%(-i) / (-2i) e*(-i) = cos 1- i sinl Res[f, -i] = (cos 1- i sin 1) / (-2i) (sin 1)/2 + i(cos 1)/2

Example 3 — Residue of f(z) = z / (z? — 5z + 6) at each simple pole Given: f(z) = z / (z? - 5z + 6) Step 1 — Factorise the denominator. 2? — 5z + 6 = (z — 2)(z — 3) HM simple poles at z = 2 andz =3. Step 2 — Apply Formula 2 (p(z) = z, q(z) = z? — 5z + 6, q'(z) = 2z — 5): Res[f, 2] p(2)/q' (2) 2 / (2*2 - 5) -2 Res[f, 3] P(3)/q' (3) 3 / (2*3 - 5) 3 Step 3 - Verification via partial fractions. z / [(z-2) (z-3)] = A/(z-2) + B/(z-3) Multiply through: z = A(z-3) + B(z-2) Set z 2: A(-1) —2 (matches Res[f,2]) Set z 3 B(1) 3 (matches Res[f,3]) V Example 4 — Residue of f(z) = cos(z) / [z(z? + 4)] atz =0 Given: f(z) = cos z / [z(z? + 4)] Step 1 — Identify poles. Denominator zeros: z = 0 (simple pole), z = +2i (simple poles). Step 2 — Residue at z = 0 using Formula 3 (write f(z) = g(z)/z with g(z) = cos z / (z? + 4)): g(z) = cos(z) / (z*2 + 4) Res[f, 0] = g(0) = cos(0) / (0+ 4) = ™@ The residue at z = 0 is 1/4. The residues at z = +2i can similarly be found using Formula 2.

6. Applications of Residue at Simple Pole The computation of residues at simple poles finds direct application in several important areas: @ Evaluation of Definite Integrals: Real integrals of the form J R(cos 6, sin 6) dé.and improper integrals | f(x) dx from —co to +c0 are evaluated by closing the contour in the complex plane and applying the Residue Theorem. @ Inverse Laplace Transform: The Bromwich integral formula for the inverse Laplace transform requires residue computation at simple (and higher-order) poles of F(s). @ Signal Processing & Control Systems: Poles of transfer functions H(s) determine system stability. Residues give the amplitude of each exponential mode in the partial-fraction expansion. @ Fluid Mechanics & Electrostatics: Complex potential functions with simple poles model source/sink flows; residues give the strength of the singularity. 7. Important Theorems & Corollaries Theorem (Cauchy's Integral Formula): If f is analytic inside and on C and zi is inside C, then: (closed integral over C) £(z)/(z-z0) dz = 2*pi*i * £(z0) lf This is the residue formula in disguise: Res[f/(z-z0), z0] = f(z0). Theorem (Jordan's Lemma): Useful for closing contours in the upper/lower half-plane when evaluating improper integrals involving e'°“f(z) with a > 0. The integral over the semicircular arc > 0 as R > ~ provided |f(z)| — 0 uniformly. 8. Summary fintee tosouen roms Limit method (Formula 1) Any simple pole lim_{z->z0} (z-z0)f(z) Quotient method (Formula 2) — f = p/q, q(z0)=0, q'(z0)#0 p(z0) / q'(z0) Direct substitution (Formula 3) f = g(z)/(z-z0), g analytic g(z0)

9. Diagram — Contour Integration with Simple Poles The diagram below illustrates a typical contour C enclosing two simple poles zl and zl. The Residue Theorem converts the contour integral into a sum of residues. Im(z) zm (simple pole) e _C f(z)dz = 2ni - [Res(f, Zl) + Res(f,z™)] 10. References NPTEL Video Lecture: Complex Analysis — Residue Theorem and Applications URL: https://nptel.ac.in/courses/111/107/111107132/ Instructor: Prof. P. A. S. Sree Krishna, IIT Guwahati Churchill, R. V. & Brown, J. W. (2013). Complex Variables and Applications (9th ed.). McGraw-Hill. Ahlifors, L. V. (1979). Complex Analysis (3rd ed.). McGraw-Hill. Kreyszig, E. (2011). Advanced Engineering Mathematics (10th ed.). John Wiley & Sons. End of Report - Self Learning Activity — 3 - Residue at Simple Pole (Calculation)
